subject: Physics
title: Dark Matter: The Invisible Mass
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Dark Matter: The Invisible Mass.

hook.text: 85% of the matter in the universe is invisible. It doesn't emit, absorb, or reflect light.

buildup.text: In the 1930s, Fritz Zwicky noticed galaxies in clusters move too fast to be held by visible matter alone.

discovery.text: Vera Rubin confirmed it in the 1970s. Galaxy rotation curves showed stars orbit too fast—something unseen adds mass.

twist.text: We can see dark matter's gravitational effects. It bends light from distant galaxies—gravitational lensing.

climax.text: Despite decades of searching, no one has directly detected a dark matter particle. Its nature is unknown.

punchline.text: We know it's there. We can see its pull. But we can't see it.

quiz.question: How do we know dark matter exists?
options: Its gravitational effects on visible matter and light are measurable
We've captured dark matter particles in a lab
It emits faint radio signals we can detect
